frameworks.name: Occam's razor
frameworks.one_line: Normative principle balancing fit and simplicity in model selection

frameworks.name: predictive model selection
frameworks.one_line: How humans choose between competing predictive models given data

frameworks.name: statistically optimal benchmark
frameworks.one_line: Normative complexity-penalized benchmark against which human choices are compared

core_question: Do humans follow Occam's razor when selecting between competing predictive models?
key_assumptions: Participants can inspect underlying data used to fit each model
A statistically optimal benchmark exists for model selection
Model complexity and fit can be dissociated in choice tasks
manipulations: Choices between competing predictive models of varying complexity
Visibility of the underlying data used to fit the models
Noise level and amount of data available for fitting
key_findings: Participants systematically neglected model complexity relative to the optimal benchmark
Participants often preferred models that overfit the data
Participants partially compensated by adjusting decision thresholds
Threshold adjustment failed to fully account for simpler misspecified models outperforming complex ones
interpretations: Simplicity is not a general cognitive preference in predictive model selection
When predicting, people prefer a good fit over appropriate complexity penalization
